title: Cat Excessive Licking of Genitals: Causes and When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Is your cat licking their genital area excessively? Learn about causes like UTIs, when it becomes an emergency, and how to triage your pet at home.
content_html:
What is Excessive Genital Licking?

While cats are fastidious groomers, excessive licking focused specifically on the genital or urinary area is often a sign of underlying discomfort, irritation, or pain. This behavior frequently indicates that your cat is trying to soothe an area affected by inflammation, infection, or a physical blockage. Common triggers include urinary tract infections (UTIs), feline lower urinary tract disease (FLUTD), or skin-related allergies.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for excessive genital licking is typically Medium. It warrants a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours to prevent the condition from worsening. However, if your cat is male and is also straining to urinate, vocalizing in pain, or unable to produce any urine, this is a Life-Threatening Emergency known as a urinary blockage. In such cases, you must seek an emergency animal hospital immediately.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear, well-lit photo of the genital area can be incredibly helpful for a veterinary triage professional. A photo allows the vet to look for clinical signs such as redness, swelling, discharge, or hair loss. This visual evidence helps determine if the issue is a surface-level skin irritation or if it suggests a more serious internal inflammatory process, allowing for a more accurate recommendation on how quickly your pet needs to be seen.

faqs.question: Can stress cause my cat to lick their genitals?
faqs.answer: Yes, cats may engage in over-grooming or 'psychogenic alopecia' due to environmental stress or anxiety, but medical causes must be ruled out first.

faqs.question: What are the signs of a urinary tract infection in cats?
faqs.answer: Common signs include frequent trips to the litter box, small amounts of urine, blood in the urine, and excessive licking of the genital region.

faqs.question: Should I put a cone on my cat to stop the licking?
faqs.answer: An Elizabethan collar (cone) can prevent further self-trauma and inflammation until you can see a veterinarian, but it will not treat the underlying cause of the discomfort.
